text: Climate change is driven by human activities that amplify the natural "greenhouse effect". By burning fossil fuels, cutting down forests, and engaging in intensive agriculture, humanity has released unprecedented amounts of heat-trapping gases into the atmosphere, causing global temperatures to rise. Welcome to the United Nations +2The primary drivers of this warming fall into the following core categories: Burning Fossil Fuels (68% of emissions): Coal, oil, and natural gas used for electricity generation, transportation, and industrial manufacturing are the largest contributors to global warming, releasing massive amounts of carbon dioxide (CO2cap C cap O sub 2𝐶𝑂2).Deforestation: Forests act as natural carbon sinks, absorbing CO2cap C cap O sub 2𝐶𝑂2. When trees are cut down or burned, this stored carbon is released back into the atmosphere, diminishing the Earth's capacity to regulate greenhouse levels.Agriculture and Livestock: Intensive farming practices emit significant quantities of powerful greenhouse gases. Methane (CH4cap C cap H sub 4𝐶𝐻4) is produced by livestock during digestion and from manure, while nitrous oxide (N2Ocap N sub 2 cap O𝑁2𝑂) is heavily released through the use of synthetic fertilizers.Industrial Processes: Manufacturing sectors (such as cement and steel production) require immense amounts of energy and rely on chemical reactions that directly emit CO2cap C cap O sub 2𝐶𝑂2 and highly potent fluorinated gases. Welcome to the United Nations +4Learn more about the science behind these drivers and how they are impacting the planet in the United Nations Causes and Effects Overview or review the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) reports on the NASA Climate Change Science platform.
